title: Best Hammer Drills 2026: Rotary Hammers That Drill Concrete
excerpt: From corded to cordless, find the best hammer drills (and rotary hammers) that power through concrete, brick, and stone for home improvement projects.
author: Editorial Team
metaDescription: From corded to cordless, find the best hammer drills (and rotary hammers) that power through concrete, brick, and stone for home improvement projects.
intro:
If a standard pistol-grip hammer drill has ever left you sweating over a single hole in concrete, you already know the problem: it lacks the piston-driven impact of a rotary hammer. An SDS-Plus rotary hammer drives a bit with focused percussive force, making short work of masonry that bogs down ordinary drills. This guide cuts through the terminology to help you select the tool that matches your actual hole size and material.

Corded rotary hammers deliver nonstop power and a lower cost, but demand an outlet. Cordless SDS tools sacrifice some stamina for the mobility to climb ladders or work in unfinished spaces without a cord. We’ve weighed these trade-offs across real-world performance, so you can choose confidently between a compact 12V subcompact for small anchors and a heavy-duty 13-amp demolition machine.

products.title: BOSCH 11255VSR Bulldog Xtreme 8 Amp 1 Inch Corded Variable Speed SDS-Plus Concrete/Masonry Rotary Hammer
products.label: Bosch 11255VSR Bulldog
products.badge: Best Overall Hammer Drill for Concrete
products.oneLiner: Corded 8-Amp SDS rotary with 2.0 ft-lbs impact and multi-mode operation for consistent concrete drilling.
products.usageNote: 💡 Tip: Pair with a heavy-gauge extension cord to gain flexibility while maintaining full motor power.
pros: Motors through concrete without bogging down, even in reinforced slabs — 86 mentions, 93% positive on motor power
Quickly drills up to 1-inch holes in brick and stone for anchors and conduit — 113 mentions, 93% positive on concrete cutting
Mid-range price delivers performance that competes with costlier corded and cordless models — 96 mentions, 93% positive on value
cons: Brush wear can require replacement after prolonged heavy use — 103 durability mentions, 23% negative — brush wear noted
Corded design confines you to sites with outlet access, limiting remote work — Expected tradeoff for corded power
products.verdict: If you can work near an outlet and want consistent concrete drilling without investing in a battery platform, this rotary hammer sets the standard.
products.review:
The 8 Amp motor spins up an SDS-Plus chuck that powers through concrete at 2.0 ft-lbs of impact energy without stalling, even in reinforced slabs. Drilling 1-inch holes for conduit or setting anchors in brick feels rapid and controlled, and the tool handles light chiseling without overheating. At 10.4 lbs, it provides enough heft to stabilize the bit but stays manageable for downward drilling, and the variable speed trigger lets you start holes cleanly. A three-mode selector switches between rotation-only, hammer-drill, and hammer-only, so you are not locked into one function.

This rotary hammer fits homeowners tackling fence posts or masonry repairs and electricians roughing in boxes, provided a power outlet is nearby. The corded design means no battery anxiety and a simpler internal layout, but it does confine you to jobsites with accessible power. Occasional brush replacement may be needed after years of heavy use, though the fix is inexpensive and straightforward. Ultimately, it suits anyone from a weekend warrior mounting a TV on brick to a contractor sinking Tapcons all day, as long as a cord reel reaches the work area.

💡 💡 Tip: Pair with a heavy-gauge extension cord to gain flexibility while maintaining full motor power.

products.review:
Unlike hybrid hammer drill/drivers that tack on a hammer mode, the DCH273B is a dedicated SDS-Plus rotary hammer engineered for concrete and masonry. It powers through 3/8-inch and 1/2-inch anchor holes in reinforced slabs without bogging down, and the SHOCKS system noticeably reduces vibration during extended overhead work.

This bare tool makes immediate sense for electricians, plumbers, and HVAC techs who already own DEWALT 20V batteries and need a lightweight rotary for anchoring and occasional light chipping. Without existing batteries, the added cost pushes it well above corded alternatives like the top pick — a tradeoff justified only if cordless mobility is a hard requirement.

💡 💡 Tip: Pair with a 5Ah or larger DEWALT 20V battery to keep pace through batches of 1/2-inch holes; smaller packs drain quickly during sustained hammering.

products.review:
Weighing just 2.18 pounds, the Milwaukee 3404-20 M12 Fuel slips into tight stud bays where bulkier drills can't. The 1/2-inch all-metal chuck and 400 in-lbs of torque deliver enough punch to set Tapcons and small concrete anchors without straining.

For electricians and HVAC techs running anchor holes in confined spaces, the compact size is the real advantage. The trade-off: continuous hammering drains the M12 battery quickly, and it cannot bore holes larger than 1/2 inch in concrete. This is a specialist for precision anchoring, not a substitute for a full-size rotary hammer.

💡 💡 Tip: Keep a couple of spare M12 batteries nearby; hammer mode depletes the compact packs faster than driving screws.

Unlike rotary hammer picks that focus on longevity, this kit prioritizes immediate accessory value. The 100-piece bit set plus a case, side handle, and depth gauge eliminate separate purchases for drilling into brick and driving screws. The 7.5 Amp motor has enough power for occasional concrete holes, but sustained use can push it past its limits.

It suits homeowners tackling a few anchor holes or small masonry projects, who accept that the motor may smoke or fail under continuous use. The low price makes it a low-risk entry point for learning if a hammer drill meets your needs without overinvesting. For regular or heavy-duty concrete drilling, a dedicated rotary hammer is a more dependable long-term choice.

💡 💡 Tip: Verify all components are present before starting your project; missing chuck key or side handle can be replaced but it saves time to check early.

buyingGuide.heading: Impact Energy (Joules)
buyingGuide.body:
Impact energy determines how fast a rotary hammer can fracture concrete. For a corded rotary, 2 ft-lbs (about 2.7 Joules) easily handles 3/8-inch holes; 7 Joules (roughly 5 ft-lbs) powers through 1-inch holes and speeds demolition. Cordless models often quote Joules differently, so pay attention to real-world drilling speeds rather than only the number.

The hidden trade-off: higher impact adds weight and cost. Match Joules to the anchor size you drill most often—overspending on top-tier impact makes no sense if you only set 1/4-inch anchors.

buyingGuide.heading: Corded vs. Cordless
buyingGuide.body:
Corded rotary hammers never fade mid-job and are typically cheaper, but tether you to an outlet—a non-issue for basement or garage work, but limiting on a roof or unpowered jobsite.

Cordless models use 18V/20V max batteries for lag-free mobility; however, runtime drops sharply under continuous hammering, and spare batteries add ongoing cost. Choose corded when you work near power and value simplicity, or cordless when you need to move frequently between locations without managing extension cords.

buyingGuide.heading: SDS-Plus vs. Keyed Chuck
buyingGuide.body:
An SDS-Plus chuck grips the bit with spring-loaded balls, allowing the bit to slide axially and deliver concentrated percussion. This design enables fast one-handed bit changes and prevents the slow loosening that plagues standard chucks under vibration.

Keyed or keyless chucks hold round-shank bits, but hammer action can cause slippage or heat buildup. For concrete masonry, SDS-Plus is the standard; a regular chuck works for occasional small holes but becomes a frustration point in anything more than very light duty.

buyingGuide.heading: Weight and Handling
buyingGuide.body:
Lighter rotary hammers (under 5 lbs) excel for overhead anchor setting and all-day comfort, but they trade off impact energy and often omit a chiseling mode. Heavier models (10+ lbs) pack higher Joules and stand up to demolition, yet quickly fatigue your arms when working above shoulder height.

Consider how much of your work is horizontal versus upward—if you drill mostly into walls at chest level, weight matters less; if you spend hours over your head, a compact SDS rated around 2 Joules will save your shoulders.

buyingGuide.heading: Kits and Bare Tools
buyingGuide.body:
Many SDS rotary hammers ship as bare tools, meaning no battery, charger, or bits. If you already own a compatible battery platform, buying bare saves money; if not, building a new battery system adds significant upfront cost.

Kits that toss in quality SDS bits, chisels, and a carrying case add immediate value and prevent steep accessory purchases later. Always check whether a tool-only listing fits your existing ecosystem, or whether a bundle price makes more sense for your start-up gear.

faq.question: What is the difference between a hammer drill and a rotary hammer?
faq.answer: A hammer drill adds a small axial tap to a spinning chuck; it's fine for occasional small holes in brick but struggles in hard concrete. A rotary hammer uses an internal piston to deliver powerful, focused blows independent of the rotation, enabling much faster drilling in concrete and stone. For anything beyond a few anchor holes, an SDS-Plus rotary hammer is the right choice.

faq.question: Why is my hammer drill not drilling into concrete even with a masonry bit?
faq.answer: A standard pistol-grip hammer drill often lacks the impact energy to fracture dense concrete aggregate. The chuck can't transfer strong blows effectively, causing the bit to overheat and glaze. Switching to an SDS-Plus rotary hammer with at least 1.5 Joules of impact typically solves this, even when using the same bit tip.

faq.question: Can I use a cordless hammer drill for concrete anchor holes?
faq.answer: Yes, but only small holes (up to 1/4 or 3/8 inch) in brick or light block. A cordless hammer drill driver works for limited tasks; for repeated concrete anchoring, a cordless SDS rotary hammer provides far better speed and prevents battery overload. If you anticipate more than a dozen holes, invest in a dedicated SDS tool.

faq.question: How many Joules of impact energy do I need for a 1-inch hole in concrete?
faq.answer: For a 1-inch hole in poured concrete, aim for at least 3 Joules (about 2.2 ft-lbs) in a corded rotary hammer. Cordless models with 2.0–2.6 Joules can manage but will be noticeably slower. Impact energy below 2 Joules will struggle and risk overheating the tool.
